Event: He bullies other kids at school for enjoyment
Explanation: He gains status/enjoyment by repeatedly hurting classmates physically and socially.

Event: He becomes the subordinate of Jimbo in the bully hierarchy
Explanation: Yielding leadership lowers his dominance with no clear gain while not much affecting others.

Event: He falls in love with female bullies
Explanation: Mutual attraction with peers potentially benefits both sides without evident harm.

Event: He jumps out of a moving bus to avoid discussing his sexuality
Explanation: He seriously endangers himself just to escape an awkward conversation, benefitting no one.

Event: He admits he misses Mrs. Krabappel after her death
Explanation: Expressing grief/attachment is emotionally healthy for him and honors a dead teacher.

Event: He taunts people in Bart’s social circle with occasional punches
Explanation: He still gets amusement/status from mild bullying at the expense of others’ comfort.

Event: He befriends Bart over time
Explanation: Friendship with Bart yields mutual support and shared fun.

Event: He dates Lisa in later seasons
Explanation: A consensual relationship is portrayed as mutually positive.

Event: He reverts to being an antagonist to Bart and Lisa in some episodes
Explanation: He regains bully benefits while causing them stress or harm.

Event: He laughs at anyone who has something embarrassing happen, wherever they are
Explanation: He relentlessly seeks amusement from others’ misfortune, providing them only discomfort.

Event: He takes offense to Bart “learning on his own” and retaliates
Explanation: He punishes Bart for trivial/positive behavior, harming Bart more than himself.

Event: He bullies others because he enjoys it, despite his rough home life
Explanation: Regardless of causes, he derives pleasure while inflicting direct harm.

Event: He works with Martin when circumstances force cooperation
Explanation: Reluctant teamwork still helps them achieve shared goals.

Event: He loves and remains loyal to his mother despite her shortcomings
Explanation: Affection for a flawed parent sustains a key relationship for both.

Event: He has a positive relationship with Marge as a parental substitute
Explanation: He gains guidance and care while giving Marge a meaningful mentoring role.

Event: He punched Bart for “besmirching an innocent girl’s name”
Explanation: He sacrifices himself (risking trouble) to uphold a principle, but still harms Bart.

Event: He attacks Bart for wasting the teacher’s time
Explanation: He imposes his idea of justice via violence, harming Bart more than helping class.

Event: He attacks Bart for taking credit for others’ work
Explanation: He aggressively enforces fairness in a way that injures Bart without real due process.

Event: He writes an impassioned essay against flag burning
Explanation: Expressing a thoughtful stance boosts his standing and contributes to civic discourse.

Event: He refuses to pick on disabled kids
Explanation: He limits his own bullying “fun” to protect a vulnerable group.

Event: He offers to protect a blind kid from bullies
Explanation: He uses his strength and reputation to defend someone at risk.

Event: He offers sympathy when Milhouse’s parents split
Explanation: He provides emotional support at some cost to his tough image.

Event: He takes pride that his grandfather sentenced 47 men to death
Explanation: He boosts his self-image from an ancestor’s extreme harm to others.

Event: He uses his planning ability to help Marge organize ending mixed wrestling
Explanation: He applies intelligence to help Marge achieve a school-level policy change.

Event: He tells Principal Skinner to shut up when his bad grades are announced
Explanation: He shows disrespect that risks punishment while only briefly silencing Skinner.

Event: He admits after losing to Bart that he wants to be an event planner
Explanation: Honestly stating his aspiration helps him and invites supportive responses.

Event: He accepts Marge’s suggestion he could plan Lisa’s wedding and says “I’d like that”
Explanation: He embraces a prosocial future role connecting him with Lisa and the family.

Event: He becomes obsessed with Bart when he realizes he has no friends
Explanation: Clinging intensely to one person strains Bart and leaves Nelson socially fragile.

Event: He lies to Lisa and goes back to bullying after she tries to reform him
Explanation: He preserves his bully identity at the cost of Lisa’s trust and feelings.

Event: He continues bullying after Bart reunites his family
Explanation: He keeps beating Bart despite Bart helping him, gaining status while betraying reciprocity.

Event: He misleads Skinner with tough talk about beating someone up while discussing huckleberries
Explanation: He protects a “soft” interest by maintaining his tough facade with no clear harm.

Event: He excels at Home Economics while failing other subjects
Explanation: Focusing on a strength benefits him and may indirectly benefit others later.

Event: He cheers passionately for Andy Williams, especially “Moon River”
Explanation: Fandom gives him joy and contributes to the shared concert atmosphere.

Event: He becomes a reformed bully and friend to Bart in later seasons
Explanation: Reducing bullying and forming friendships benefits everyone.

Event: He joins Jimbo’s Gang of Bullies as a frequent fourth member
Explanation: Being part of a bully group enhances his status while hurting multiple targets.

Event: He smokes and drinks at a young age
Explanation: Substance use seriously harms him and provides little or no benefit to others.

Event: He often threatens or beats up his own friends when annoyed
Explanation: He maintains dominance and fear at the cost of his friends’ wellbeing.

Event: He goes to live with the Simpsons for a time
Explanation: Staying with a stable family helps him and enriches the Simpsons’ dynamic.

Event: He bullies/pranks Sherri and Terri to defend Lisa from their teasing
Explanation: He protects Lisa but still harms others via bullying tactics.

Event: He kicks Milhouse through the fence hole to help him sneak in
Explanation: The kick is rough but functional, enabling Milhouse to join the group.

Event: He pulls the fence back to make it easier for Lisa to crawl through
Explanation: He directly helps Lisa with minimal cost to himself.

Event: He befriends the blind kid Kevin and vows to destroy anyone who bullies him
Explanation: He uses his fearsome reputation to protect Kevin and gains a valued bond.

Event: He tries to comfort Milhouse when his parents divorce
Explanation: Again he offers empathy at some reputational cost to his tough image.

Event: He tells a butler to stop “buttling himself”
Explanation: He mocks/annoys the butler for a laugh, with minor benefit to himself.

Event: He puts an owl in a headlock and tells it to stop endangering itself
Explanation: He risks getting hurt and stresses the animal without any meaningful gain.

Event: He laughs at Lisa so frequently that she says his mocking has lost its power
Explanation: Overuse of mockery still bothers her, though it starts losing effectiveness.

Event: He reluctantly agrees that his mocking has lost its power
Explanation: Realizing his main “weapon” is less effective hurts his status but offers others relief.

Event: He uses “Parent Never Came Back from the Store” as an excuse for his own kids in a flash-forward
Explanation: Reusing a hurtful abandonment excuse shields him somewhat but passes harm downward.

Event: He smokes and drinks despite being a child (reiterated as troubling behavior)
Explanation: The behavior remains self-destructive over time.

Event: He helps Lisa understand why bullies pick on nerds in “Bye, Bye, Nerdie”
Explanation: He shares insider knowledge that aids Lisa’s understanding and possibly his own self-awareness.
